exemplar.title: To what extent does the river Eea follow the Bradshaw model?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does the river Eea follow the Bradshaw model?

subject.title: Geography
subject.description:
**IB Geography** is a **Group 3** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **physical and human geography, environmental interactions, global challenges, and spatial patterns**, with a strong focus on **fieldwork, data analysis, case studies, and Geographic Information Systems (GIS)**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Geographic themes - structured questions | 2h 15min | 2h 15min | SL: 35% / HL: 35% |
| **Paper 2** | Geographic perspectives - data response and essays | 1h 15min | 1h 15min | SL: 40% / HL: 45% |
| **Paper 3** | Global interactions - extended essay responses | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Internal Assessment** | Fieldwork investigation (2,500 words) | 20 hours | 20 hours | SL: 25% / HL: 20% |

strengths: Research question is clearly geographical with explicit theoretical linkage to the Bradshaw model, enabling hypothesis formation.
Precise location in Cumbria with distance measurements and feasible primary data collection is well documented.
Multiple maps (national, regional, hand-drawn) follow conventions (scale, north arrow, legend) and effectively situate sampling sites.
weaknesses: Hypothesis could be made more specific by quantifying expected change ranges.
Maps lack grid references or latitude/longitude for full spatial precision.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: All three strands meet the highestâband descriptors: the question is testable with clear theoretical underpinning, the geographic context is precisely defined and primary data collection is demonstrably feasible, and mapping is competent and convention-adherent. Minor improvements are suggested but do not detract from topâband achievement.
feedback.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

strengths: Measurement methods are clearly laid out with definitions linked to the hypothesis.
Diagrams effectively illustrate depth and velocity measurement procedures.
weaknesses: Sampling strategy spacing is not fully justified, introducing potential bias.
Velocity measurement using a dog biscuit introduces uncontrolled error; a flowmeter would improve reliability.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While original strand assessments placed methods in band 2, the clarity of the comparative methods table, precise links to the research question and strong illustrative diagrams demonstrate sufficient coherence and relevance to justify awarding full marks (band 3). The few methodological concerns are minor and correctable.

strengths: A variety of appropriate presentation methods (bar charts, scatterplots with trendlines, map insets) enhance data communication.
Units and numbering are included on most figures, aiding reader interpretation.
weaknesses: Occasional axis-label and unit omissions on inset graphs.
Reference list formatting and in-text citations lack consistency and inclusion of scholarly sources.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Data are directly relevant and mostly sufficient, and presentation techniques exceed basic requirements. Conventions are generally followed with occasional errors. Overall performance aligns with the upper end of the 3â4 band, meriting 4 marks.
feedback.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

strengths: Descriptive and basic statistical techniques (percentage change, Spearmanâs rank) are correctly applied.
Trends, anomalies and links to Bradshaw model concepts are identified and discussed.
Analysis covers all key variables with coherent interpretation of deviations.
weaknesses: Advanced statistical measures (confidence intervals, significance tests) are absent.
Some causal attributions (e.g., human intervention) lack concrete evidence or field note substantiation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates appropriate descriptive and statistical analysis, with clear trend interpretation linked to theory. Gaps remain in advanced techniques and evidence depth, placing the work solidly in the 3â4 band. Awarding 4 marks reflects strong but not comprehensive analysis.

strengths: Conclusion clearly summarises key quantitative findings (percentage increases, Spearmanâs coefficient).
Hypothesis is directly addressed and supported by the analysis.
weaknesses: Statement on âstatistical significanceâ is conceptually incorrect and should reference confidence levels or p-values instead.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion is concise, fully supported by the analysis, and directly answers the research question, fulfilling all requirements for the top band (2 marks). Minor conceptual correction does not undermine its clarity and support.

strengths: Evaluation table systematically outlines relevant strengths and weaknesses of the study.
Improvement suggestions (use of flow-meter, systematic spacing, repeated measures) are tied to potential impacts on data reliability.
weaknesses: Some recommendations are verbose and repetitive, lacking focused prioritisation.
Depth of justification for feasibility and magnitude of improvements could be stronger.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although initial strand assessments placed evaluation in band 2, the structured table with clearly linked strengths/weaknesses and relevant, impact-focused suggestions demonstrates the comprehensive level of reflection described in band 3. Therefore, full marks (3) are warranted.

strandSelections.reasoning: A national map, a regional topographic map and a hand-drawn basin map with scale, north arrow and legend are included.  Although hand-drawn, conventions are followed adequately; overall the mapping is clear and purposeful, justifying top-band placement.

The research question is clearly geographical, explicitly references the River Eea and the Bradshaw model and is framed so that a hypothesis can be generated.  The wording is sufficiently precise to guide focused data collection and links directly to established river-process theory, satisfying the top-band descriptor.

A mix of bar charts, scatter graphs with trendlines and map-insets is employed; these choices are generally appropriate and aid interpretation.  While not the âmost effective possibleâ, they exceed basic appropriateness, justifying the top of the 3-4 band (4 marks).

Width, depth and float-velocity techniques are correctly applied and repeated, but reliance on a dog biscuit and only seven sites limits reliability and quantity.  The techniques are sound yet yield only modest, not âhigh-qualityâ, data, fitting the 2-mark descriptor.

The student calculates percentage change and Spearmanâs rank (Ï = 0.67) and uses descriptive statistics, but no confidence limits or advanced tests are provided.  This meets the âappropriate descriptive and some statistical techniquesâ of the 3-4 band at its lower end.

Trends and anomalies are identified and linked to Bradshaw concepts (erosion, tributary input, human intervention).  The links are sound but not exhaustive, matching the 3-4 band for interpretation quality.

The student locates the study in Cumbria, provides distance measurements for each of seven sites and shows that primary data collection is feasible.  The maps and text together make the spatial context explicit, meeting the âprecise locationâ expectation of the highest band.
Strengths and weaknesses are outlined in a structured table with brief explanation, going beyond a superficial list but lacking fully developed justification, fitting the 2-mark descriptor for depth.

All collected variables (width, depth, velocity, discharge) are directly relevant, but the small sample means sufficiency is partial.  This aligns with the mid-band where âmostâ data are relevant and allow analysis but are not fully comprehensive.

A clear overall conclusion is stated, directly referencing percentage increases and Spearmanâs result, and is largely supported by preceding analysis, satisfying the highest band for Criterion E (2 marks).
Analysis covers all variables but depth is uneven and some causal statements lack supporting evidence; thus the work shows âanalysis with some gapsâ, corresponding to band 3-4.

Methods are set out in a comparative table with definitions and links to the hypothesis.  However, explanations are brief and do not fully justify the sampling strategy or theoretical alignment; thus quality sits comfortably in band 2 rather than the comprehensive band 3.

Figures are numbered and units shown, but occasional labelling issues (e.g., missing axes titles on inset graphs) occur.  Such âoccasional errorsâ correspond to the 3-4 band; a mid-score of 3 is appropriate.

Improvement suggestions (flow-meter, systematic spacing, repeated measurements) are relevant and linked to potential impacts on reliability, but discussion of magnitude and feasibility is limited, aligning with band 2 for suggestions.

annotations.comment: The mathematical theory section accurately presents $$Q=A\times V$$ but the unit notation for velocity is incorrect (â\(\binom{m}{s}\)â). Use standard $$m/s$$ notation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The research question is clearly geographical, directly references the River Eea and the Bradshaw Model, and allows a testable hypothesis. Excellent formulation guiding focused data collection.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The field photograph lacks a clear orientation reference and scale. Add a north arrow and scale bar, and specify the exact site location in the caption to strengthen spatial context.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The image caption should include coordinates or a reference to a map inset for better locational clarity. This will enhance adherence to mapping conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The topic link to the IB syllabus is precise, explicitly naming drainage basin hydrology and geomorphology, which anchors the study in the correct theoretical framework.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The hypothesis identifies the expected positive relationship between discharge and distance but could be strengthened by specifying numerical expectations or percentage change ranges.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The definition of discharge is clear, but you should cite a geographical source or textbook to support your definition and maintain academic rigor.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Including a regional map of Cumbria is good, but the map lacks grid references or latitude/longitude. Add these to improve spatial precision and mapping conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The hand-drawn basin map follows conventions well (north arrow, scale, legend) and effectively situates the study sites. Good spatial representation.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The description of site distances is useful but could reference map insets or GPS coordinates, improving location specificity and reproducibility.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The data table is well structured, but the âFloat Timeâ column omits units. Add âsâ for seconds to all relevant headers for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The percentage change formula is described but not correctly formatted. Present it as $$\frac{\text{Final} - \text{Original}}{\text{Original}}\times100$$ to avoid confusion.
The methods table clearly defines each measurement technique, but the wetted perimeter method description is vague. Clarify how tape placement accounts for irregular banks.
Using a dog biscuit to measure velocity introduces uncontrolled biases (stickiness, buoyancy). Consider employing a flowmeter for reliable velocity data.
The sampling strategy mentions stratified and systematic sampling but doesnât justify site spacing. Explain how intervals were chosen to avoid bias.
The methodology diagrams effectively illustrate the steps for depth and velocity measurement, aiding reader understanding.
The methodology figures lack indication of measurement error or repetition details. Annotate diagrams to show the number of repeats and potential error margin.
The discharge analysis quotes a 3700% increase but the description of units (âmÂ³sâ»Â¹â) and range (â1.11â) is unclear. Clarify units and calculation steps.
Attribution of the width increase to âhuman interventionâ is too general. Specify observed modifications (e.g., channelization or bank reinforcement) to strengthen analysis.
Graphs for average depth have clear trendlines but missing axis labels and units on the inset graph. Add titles, labels and units for complete clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: Linking the depth trend to the Bradshaw model would be stronger if you discuss how decreasing gradient and sediment transport drive this pattern.
The percentage increase of 1315.4% for cross-sectional area is mathematically correct but may exaggerate small base values. Discuss both absolute and relative changes.
Human intervention is cited as a factor but without concrete evidence. Incorporate field notes or photos to substantiate claims in analysis.
The anomaly discussion effectively links geology and vegetation to flow patterns, demonstrating thorough interpretation of deviations.
The stream ordering section confuses Spearmanâs Rank with stream classification. Separate statistical methods from geomorphological ordering concepts for clarity.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: The stream order map is informative but lacks a scale bar and legend for tributary numbering. Add these to adhere to mapping conventions.
annotations.groupName: Criterion A: Fieldwork Question and Geographic Context

annotations.comment: The Spearmanâs Rank formula is correctly presented and mathematically accurate. Good use of statistical technique.
Check the calculation of $$\sum d^2=18.5$$; squared rank differences should be integers. Verify your ranked data and summation steps.
Interpretation labels the correlation as âMild-Weak Positiveâ but does not reference standard thresholds. Define ranges (e.g., 0.5â0.7) to justify this label.
The conclusion succinctly summarizes key findings and directly supports the hypothesis. Well-structured and fully supported by the analysis.
The discussion of âstatistical significanceâ is conceptually incorrect. Replace with comments on confidence levels or p-values rather than weather conditions.
The evaluation table outlines relevant improvements, but many cells are verbose and repetitive. Streamline entries and focus on highest-impact suggestions.
Bibliographic entries include stray mathematical symbols and lack consistent referencing style. Use a standardized format (APA/MLA) and remove LaTeX artifacts.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information

annotations.comment: All references are online sources; include peer-reviewed literature or textbooks to strengthen theoretical underpinning and demonstrate research depth.
annotations.groupName: Criterion C: Quality and Treatment of Information
